era_context: The late 14th century is marked by the Hundred Years' War between England and France, the Ottoman Empire's expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans, the Ming Dynasty's consolidation in China, and the early Italian Renaissance. The Catholic Church remains unified but experiencing tensions that would lead to the Western Schism in 1378.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Americas (Aztec and Inca records exist but are sparse in European sources)

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English campaigns in Aquitaine and Gascony
events.description: In 1373, English forces under John of Gaunt conducted a major military campaign in Aquitaine and Gascony, attempting to consolidate English control over these regions. The campaign was part of the broader English strategy to maintain dominance in southwestern France. Though the campaign achieved some military successes, it did not result in a decisive victory.

sources.name: Froissart's Chronicles; English and French royal records
Historiographic consensus on Hundred Years' War chronology

events.certainty_note: The 1373 campaign is attested in Froissart and other chronicles, though specific details vary. The campaign is sometimes dated 1373–1374.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: consolidation of control; administrative reforms
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued consolidating Ming power in 1373 through administrative reforms and the centralization of authority. He was establishing the bureaucratic structures and systems of control that would define Ming governance. This period saw the elimination of regional power centers and the concentration of power in the imperial court.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's administrative reforms are well-documented in Ming records, though specific reforms in 1373 require careful reading of the Ming Shilu.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: Murad I, the Ottoman Sultan, continued expanding Ottoman territory in Anatolia and the Balkans in 1373. The Ottoman state was consolidating its power and establishing itself as a major regional force. This period saw the Ottoman Empire transition from a small principality to a significant power.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in 1373 is attested in Ottoman sources, but specific events and dates can vary. The broader pattern of Murad I's consolidation is well-established.

events.title: Tamerlane's consolidation of power in Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) was consolidating his power in Central Asia in 1373, eliminating rival khans and establishing the foundation of the Timurid Empire. He was conducting military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations to establish his authority over Transoxiana and neighboring regions.

sources.name: Timurid chronicles (Yazdi, Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi)

events.certainty_note: Timur's consolidation of power is well-attested in Islamic and Persian sources, though specific events in 1373 are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Petrarch's final years; continued influence on Italian humanism
events.description: In 1373, Petrarch was in his final year of life, continuing to write and influence the intellectual life of Italy. His works on classical texts and human achievement were shaping the development of humanism. Though he would die in 1374, his influence on the emerging Renaissance was profound and lasting.
events.certainty_note: Petrarch's activities in 1373 are documented in his surviving writings and correspondence, though the specific events of this year are not always clearly recorded.

disconfirming_evidence: The 1373 English campaign in Aquitaine is sometimes portrayed as a major military success, but it achieved limited territorial gains and did not resolve the underlying conflict. The significance of Ottoman expansion in 1373 is often exaggerated in later Ottoman historiography to support narratives of inevitable Ottoman dominance.
historiographic_note: 1373 is well-documented for Western Europe, the Ottoman Empire, and China. Italian sources on Petrarch and humanism are abundant. Ottoman and Central Asian sources exist but are often later compilations. The year is less well-documented for regions outside these areas. The surviving record emphasizes political and military events; economic and cultural history require inference from broader patterns.

graph_edges.note: English military campaigns in 1373 continued until the Treaty of Bruges in 1375
Petrarch's final year in 1373 led to his death in 1374
